name: Rust HIR → THIR → MIR Cascade
summary: rustc's three-stage cascade: HIR (desugared, name-resolved) for type checking and lints, THIR (typed, coercion-explicit) for pattern exhaustiveness, MIR (CFG, drop-elaborated) for borrow checking and codegen. Each stage discards information the next does not need — the canonical multi-level IR architecture.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: rustc's IR cascade is the canonical "moderate-complexity language with a multi-level IR" example. AST → **HIR** (desugared, name-resolved) → **THIR** (typed, coercion-explicit) → **MIR** (CFG, borrow-check substrate) → LLVM IR. Each stage discards information the next stage doesn't need; each stage runs analyses appropriate to its level.

sections.title: Per-stage roles
sections.content:
**HIR (High-Level IR)**: the desugared, name-resolved form of the AST. Macro expansion has run; `for` loops have become `loop` + `match`; type annotations are still expressions, not yet checked. HIR is the IR most type-checking, lints, and trait resolution operate on. Tree-shaped, with stable IDs assigned to every body and every binding.

**THIR (Typed HIR)**: HIR with all types explicit, all coercions made concrete, all method calls resolved to specific function bodies, and pattern matching desugared to a decision-tree form. THIR exists primarily as a stepping stone to MIR — most analyses don't operate on THIR directly. Pattern exhaustiveness checking happens here.

**MIR (Mid-Level IR)**: rustc's CFG-form IR; the borrow checker's native substrate. Every variable has explicit drop semantics, every borrow has a region, every reference carries the lifetime that reaches it. Pattern matching is fully desugared to chains of comparisons. Borrow checking, drop elaboration, const evaluation, and miri all operate on MIR.

sections.title: Design lesson — cascade pays off at moderate complexity
sections.content:
The lesson: even moderate-complexity languages benefit from multiple IRs. The cost is translation layers between them; the benefit is per-phase optimality. A two-level cascade (AST + final IR) loses the ability to run focused analyses at the right level; a four-level cascade (Rust's choice) keeps each pass's substrate appropriate for its work.

For language designers, the rule of thumb: if the language has substantial semantic complexity (ownership, effects, dependent types, advanced pattern matching), expect to need multiple IRs. The HIR → THIR → MIR shape generalises well — desugar early, type-explicit middle, CFG-form for analysis and codegen.
